title: Recognizing and honoring the work of community organizations and individuals who create and maintain services and educational programs for marginalized groups ensuring the resilience and prosperity of members of the LGBTQIA+ community.
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: This resolution honors people and groups who help LGBTQIA+ communities by creating safe places and education programs. It recognizes how important these community spaces have been throughout history. The resolution also asks Congress to keep supporting organizations that serve LGBTQIA+ people and other groups who face discrimination. These groups work to bring different communities together and connect them with local resources.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues
subjects: Social work, volunteer service, charitable organizations

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution recognizes and honors the work of community organizations and individuals who create and maintain services and educational programs for marginalized groups ensuring the resilience and prosperity of members of the LGBTQIA+ community.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: LGBTQIA+ community spaces provide critical environments where people of different sexual orientations and gender identities can express themselves honestly without facing discrimination, violence, or judgment. These spaces help people feel like they belong and support their mental and emotional health. The term LGBTQIA+ means lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, questioning or queer, intersex, asexual, and other identities. Community partnership organizations have been important in creating and keeping these spaces running through their hard work and advocacy. These spaces also welcome and support members from different racial and ethnic backgrounds. In June nineteen sixty nine, police raided the Stonewall Inn, a bar that served LGBTQIA+ people when being LGBTQIA+ was against the law. This led to six days of protests led by a diverse group including transgender women of color like Marsha P. Johnson and Sylvia Rivera. In nineteen eighty five, the Gay and Lesbian Anti-Defamation League, later called GLAAD, held its first meeting at the New York Community Center with over four hundred LGBTQIA+ people. In nineteen eighty seven, the AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power, also called ACT UP, was founded to fight discrimination against people with AIDS in politics and healthcare. Ballroom is an underground LGBTQIA+ art form that started in burlesque shows in the late eighteen hundreds and became popular in cities like New York and Chicago by the nineteen nineties. It gave teams a place to compete and helped people recognize the LGBTQIA+ community. Bars and nightclubs that serve the LGBTQIA+ community have decreased by thirty seven percent in the last ten years. They now make up only one and a half percent of all bars and clubs in the United States, with only thirty two lesbian bars and nightclubs left in the whole country. According to a twenty twenty study by the Williams Institute at UCLA Law School, sexual and gender minorities face violence at much higher rates than other people. They experience violent crimes at a rate of seventy one point one per one thousand people compared to nineteen point two per one thousand for people who are not sexual or gender minorities. A twenty twenty two study by the New York City Anti-Violence Project found that sixty two point one percent of three hundred eighty LGBTQIA+ businesses, groups, and organizations experienced at least one incident of hate violence in twenty twenty two. Nine out of ten organizations said they need security measures they do not currently have. Making sure LGBTQIA+ businesses, groups, and organizations have access to resources and money to keep supporting vulnerable people is very important. When protected spaces are attacked with violence and hatred, innocent people pay the price, often with their lives. The twenty sixteen Pulse Nightclub shooting in Orlando killed forty nine people, and the twenty twenty two Club Q shooting killed five people. Both shootings seriously damaged the sense of safety for local communities and LGBTQIA+ organizations.

sections.title: House of Representatives Resolutions
sections.text: The House of Representatives recognizes the necessity and work of those who create LGBTQIA+ community spaces with the goals of equality. It commends those who build community partnerships between marginalized groups and local resources. It acknowledges the historical importance of community building spaces in the fight against homophobic, racist, misogynistic, and hateful oppression. It honors current and past organizations and individuals dedicated to providing marginalized groups, like the LGBTQIA+ community and people of color, with space to come together and organize. It affirms the importance of education and awareness programs that highlight the historical and cultural significance of LGBTQIA+ community spaces. It encourages continued support by Congress for community organizations and individuals who continue to create community for marginalized groups.
